definition: GDP is the total dollar value of everything a country produces — every good made, every service sold — within a given period. Think of it as the economy's annual report card.
context: When GDP grows, the economy is expanding — more jobs, rising incomes, stronger corporate profits. When it shrinks for two quarters in a row, that's the textbook definition of a recession.
formula: GDP = Consumer Spending + Business Investment + Government Spending + (Exports − Imports)
insight: Q4 2025 stumbled to 1.4% — dragged by the federal government shutdown and tariff headwinds. The slowdown is expected to be temporary; economists project a bounce-back in early 2026 as those one-off drags fade and consumer spending stays resilient.
source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis · Feb 2026

definition: Prices go up over time — that's inflation. CPI measures exactly how much. It tracks a basket of everyday items — groceries, rent, gas, clothing — and shows how much more (or less) they cost compared to a year ago. When CPI climbs fast, your money buys less than it used to.
context: The Fed watches CPI closely alongside PCE. When CPI runs persistently above 2%, the Fed considers raising rates to cool demand. When it falls sharply, it may signal a weakening economy.
formula: Tracks price changes across 8 categories: Food & Beverage, Housing, Apparel, Transportation, Medical Care, Recreation, Education, and Other.
insight: Shelter costs keep headline CPI sticky above target — rent and equivalent homeowner costs make up ~35% of the index and are slow to fall. The Fed is watching core CPI closely; until it drops toward 2.5%, rate cuts remain on hold.
source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics · Feb 2026

definition: The single most powerful interest rate in the world. It's what banks charge each other for overnight loans — and it ripples through everything else: your mortgage rate, your car loan, your credit card APR. When the Fed raises it, borrowing gets more expensive across the board.
context: The FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) meets 8 times per year to review the rate. Every quarter-point move ripples through mortgages, car loans, credit cards, and bond yields within days.
formula: When FFR rises → borrowing costs increase → spending slows → inflation cools. When FFR falls → cheaper credit → spending rises → economy stimulated.
insight: The Fed is in wait-and-see mode after three cuts in late 2025. Markets have 1–2 more cuts priced in for 2026, but only if inflation cooperates. Higher rates mean bond yields stay elevated — a challenge for growth stocks but an opportunity for fixed-income investors.

definition: Every first Friday of the month, the government reveals how many jobs were added or lost — excluding farm workers. It's the most market-moving number of the month. A big miss can send stocks down hundreds of points within minutes of the release.
context: Markets treat NFP as the most market-moving data release of the month. A strong print can delay rate cuts; a weak one can accelerate them. It's the first hard look at the prior month's economic activity.
formula: NFP > 150K/month → healthy labor market. NFP < 75K/month → potential slowdown. NFP negative → jobs being cut.
insight: Payrolls are cooling from 2025's strong pace but still positive — a soft landing signal. Wage growth above 4% keeps consumer spending firm, which supports corporate earnings but also keeps the Fed cautious about cutting rates too fast.
source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics · Feb 2026

definition: Similar to CPI, but the Fed trusts it more. PCE measures what Americans actually spend on goods and services — and it adjusts when people swap expensive items for cheaper ones. That makes it a more honest picture of real-world inflation. When PCE runs hot, rate cuts get pushed back.
context: When Fed officials say 'inflation is near our target,' they're talking about PCE, not CPI. Core PCE (excluding food and energy) is the number that drives rate decisions.
formula: PCE differs from CPI: it adjusts for substitution behavior — when prices rise, consumers switch to cheaper alternatives — making it a more realistic measure of actual purchasing patterns.
insight: Core PCE above target keeps the Fed cautious, but the pace of rise matters most — it's ticking up slowly, not surging. If it stalls or reverses in coming months, the door opens for mid-2026 rate cuts, which would benefit bond holders and rate-sensitive sectors like real estate.
source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis · Jan 2026

definition: A snapshot of how much interest the government pays to borrow money at different time horizons — 2 years, 10 years, 30 years. Normally, longer loans pay higher rates. When that flips — short-term rates above long-term ones — it's called an inversion, and every U.S. recession in the past 50 years was preceded by one.
context: Every U.S. recession since 1955 was preceded by a yield curve inversion. The curve inverted in mid-2022 and remained inverted for over two years — one of the longest inversions on record.
formula: Normal: 10Y yield > 2Y yield → growth expected. Inverted: 10Y yield < 2Y yield → recession signal. Uninverting: spread returning to positive → mixed signal.
insight: The curve is finally uninverting after a record inversion — historically a mixed signal. A sustained positive spread with improving growth data points to recovery; a quick re-inversion would be a warning sign.
